text:
This review provides a comparative analysis of cross-cultural business development in the form of an applicable framework that synthesizes the economic models of the United States and emerging economies in Africa. As the modern business environment is highly globalized, companies must navigate various cultural environments to survive. By comparing the largest economy in the world, the United States of America (USA) known for its vast capital markets, high-tech innovation, and competitive business environment with the varied business environments of Africa, we derive invaluable understanding of the underlying cultural, economic and local market factors that determine business success.

We start by documenting the cultural factors unique to business. The American culture of high-stakes entrepreneurship on the one hand - as embodied by Silicon Valley or Wall Street – is vastly different from the multiple country cultures in Africa. Beyond the cultural frameworks we elucidate, the American business practices of Silicon Valley, and the professional networking sites, as well as their African counterparts are documented, whilst assessing how the competencies in each context are used to combat respective market challenges.

So why innovation matters to the strength of a nation and how this analysis links creativity, culture and competitiveness. Finally, drawing on the lessons learnt from the US and Africa, this working paper provides a practical roadmap of how firms, governments and academics can develop cross-cultural competencies and succeed globally.
